content: Existing comparisons of intrinsic curiosity modules (ICM), random network distillation (RND), and generic curious agents can be reframed to measure how each method's intrinsic reward signal aligns with the learning needs encoded in an autonomous agent's long-term episodic memory, revealing that underperforming curiosity methods often fail to prioritize novel information that fills unaddressed gaps in the agent's stored memory rather than just under-explored states. This gap-aligning curiosity mechanism can be tested as a 2025 innovation for more sample-efficient autonomous agents operating in open-ended environments.
source_topics: curiosity driven reinforcement learning ICM RND compared to curious agent
trigger_topic: curiosity driven reinforcement learning ICM RND compared to curious agent
